assessed_scope: specs 001 (core loop + ReAct) and 002 (tool executor extraction)
estimated_impact: No single item is blocking Phase 1 delivery, but 3 items (TD-001, TD-002, TD-003) will compound into architectural friction starting in Phase 2 (CodeExec strategy). The untyped model interface (TD-001) and untyped strategy signature (TD-002) will force every new strategy to rediscover duck-typing contracts by reading react.py source. The conftest duplication (TD-006) is a latent correctness risk -- if arcllm types drift, tests pass against stale mocks. Combined remediation before Phase 2 is estimated at 12-22 hours.

items.title: No Protocol/ABC for model interface
items.description: model parameter is typed as Any across run(), run_async(), react_loop(), and select_strategy(). The contract is duck-typed: must have async invoke(messages, tools=) returning an object with .content, .tool_calls, .stop_reason, .usage, .cost_usd. No Protocol defines this. Every new strategy author must reverse-engineer the contract from react.py lines 47-56. mypy cannot catch model misuse. This is the single highest-friction item for Phase 2-5 contributors.
items.remediation: Define a Protocol class ModelProtocol in types.py with invoke() signature. Replace all model: Any with model: ModelProtocol. This also documents the response shape contract (stop_reason, tool_calls, usage, cost_usd).
items.blocked_by: arcllm py.typed marker missing (TD-009). Protocol can still be defined in arcrun even without arcllm typing.

items.title: No Protocol for strategy function signature
items.description: STRATEGIES dict is typed as dict[str, Any]. Strategy functions have an implicit contract: async (model: Any, state: RunState, sandbox: Sandbox, max_turns: int) -> LoopResult. No Callable type alias or Protocol enforces this. A strategy registered with a wrong signature will only fail at runtime when called.
items.remediation: Define StrategyFn = Callable[[ModelProtocol, RunState, Sandbox, int], Awaitable[LoopResult]] type alias. Type STRATEGIES as dict[str, StrategyFn]. This catches signature mismatches at registration time.

items.title: Untyped Event.data -- all dict[str, Any]
items.description: Event.data is dict[str, Any]. Event types are raw strings ('tool.start', 'llm.call', etc.). No TypedDict or union defines what data each event type carries. Consumers must guess/check keys. This becomes painful when: (a) building dashboards/analytics over events, (b) Phase 4 hardening needs reliable event schemas for monitoring, (c) any event.data key rename silently breaks downstream consumers.
items.remediation: Option A (lightweight): Define EventType string enum + TypedDict per event type. Option B (heavier): Replace Event with union of typed dataclasses (ToolStartEvent, LLMCallEvent, etc.). Recommend Option A -- preserves current EventBus simplicity while adding type safety at emit/consume boundaries. Defer to Phase 4 unless an external consumer appears sooner.

items.title: Duplicated setup code between run() and run_async()
items.description: run() lines 30-48 and run_async() lines 67-88 repeat identical logic: validate tools, generate run_id, create EventBus, ToolRegistry, Sandbox, RunState, load strategies, select strategy, emit strategy.selected. The only difference is run() awaits the strategy directly vs run_async() wraps it in create_task. 18 lines duplicated. Any change (e.g., adding a new option, changing initialization order) must be applied in both places.
items.remediation: Extract _prepare_run(model, tools, system_prompt, task, **options) -> tuple[RunState, Sandbox, str, StrategyFn] helper. Both run() and run_async() call it, then diverge only on await vs create_task.

items.title: _build_result hardcoded to strategy_used='react'
items.description: _build_result() in react.py line 131 hardcodes strategy_used='react'. When CodeExec or Recursive strategies are added, each will need its own _build_result or accept strategy name as parameter. This is a minor scaling concern but will cause a copy-paste bug when the next strategy author copies react.py as a template.
items.remediation: Option A: Move _build_result to loop.py or a shared module, accept strategy_name parameter. Option B: Store strategy name in RunState at selection time, read it in _build_result. Option B preferred -- RunState already carries all other run metadata.

items.title: conftest.py duplicates arcllm types instead of importing them
items.description: tests/conftest.py defines its own Usage, ToolCall, TextBlock, ToolUseBlock, ToolResultBlock, and Message dataclasses that mirror arcllm.types. These are not imported from arcllm -- they are independent copies. If arcllm changes a field name, adds a field, or changes a default, tests will continue to pass against stale mock types while production code breaks. This already manifests: conftest Message has content: Any while arcllm Message likely has a more specific type. The MockModel.invoke returns conftest's LLMResponse, not arcllm's actual response type.
items.remediation: Import Message, TextBlock, ToolUseBlock, ToolResultBlock from arcllm.types (they are already used in _messages.py). Keep only Usage, ToolCall, LLMResponse, and MockModel as test-specific constructs since arcllm may not provide a ToolCall or response type. If arcllm does provide these, import them too. This ensures test fixtures stay aligned with production types.
items.blocked_by: Need to verify which types arcllm.types actually exports beyond what _messages.py uses.

items.title: STRATEGIES global dict -- thread/concurrency safety
items.description: STRATEGIES is a module-level mutable dict populated lazily by _load_strategies(). Both run() and run_async() guard with 'if not STRATEGIES: _load_strategies()'. In a concurrent scenario (multiple run() calls from different asyncio tasks), the first-call lazy load is safe because Python's GIL protects dict mutation, but the pattern is fragile. More importantly, STRATEGIES is never cleared -- if a strategy module fails to import, it will retry every call. No error handling wraps the lazy import.
items.remediation: Replace lazy-load pattern with eager registration at module import time (move import to module level, guarded by try/except). Alternatively, use a registry class with proper initialization. Low priority -- current pattern works for single-strategy Phase 1.

items.title: Token/cost accumulation uses hasattr duck typing
items.description: react.py lines 51-56 use hasattr(response, 'usage') and getattr(response, 'cost_usd', 0.0) to accumulate tokens/cost. This pattern will be duplicated in every future strategy (CodeExec, Recursive). The accumulation logic -- reading usage.input_tokens, usage.output_tokens, usage.total_tokens, and cost_usd from a response -- is strategy-independent bookkeeping that belongs in shared infrastructure.
items.remediation: Extract accumulate_usage(state, response) helper into a shared module (state.py or a new accounting.py). Each strategy calls it after model.invoke(). Eliminates hasattr/getattr pattern and ensures consistent accumulation across strategies.

items.title: arcllm missing py.typed marker -- mypy errors on every import
items.description: mypy --strict reports 3 errors, all 'Skipping analyzing arcllm.types: module is installed, but missing library stubs or py.typed marker'. This means: (a) arcrun cannot achieve clean mypy --strict, (b) all types flowing through arcllm are effectively Any from mypy's perspective, (c) type errors at the arcrun/arcllm boundary are invisible. This is an external dependency issue but has direct impact on arcrun's type safety posture.
items.remediation: Short term: add arcllm to mypy.ini [mypy-arcllm.*] ignore_missing_imports = True. Long term: add py.typed marker to arcllm package. If arcllm is first-party, this is a 5-minute fix. If third-party, open an issue.

items.title: run_async() uses **options: Any -- loses all type checking
items.description: run_async() accepts **options: Any instead of explicit keyword arguments like run() does. This means callers can pass any keyword without type error: run_async(model, tools, 'p', 't', banana=True) compiles fine. run() correctly uses explicit typed kwargs (max_turns: int, sandbox: SandboxConfig | None, etc.). The inconsistency is also a discoverability problem -- IDE autocomplete shows nothing for run_async options.
items.remediation: Match run_async() signature to run(): add explicit keyword arguments for max_turns, allowed_strategies, sandbox, on_event, transform_context. Remove **options. This aligns both entry points and enables IDE autocomplete + mypy checking.

items.title: Event type strings are raw strings with no central registry
items.description: Event types are scattered string literals: 'loop.start', 'loop.complete', 'loop.max_turns', 'turn.start', 'turn.end', 'llm.call', 'tool.start', 'tool.end', 'tool.error', 'tool.denied', 'tool.registered', 'tool.removed', 'strategy.selected'. There are 13 distinct event types across 4 files. A typo in an event type string (e.g., 'tool.stat' instead of 'tool.start') is undetectable. No consumer can enumerate available events.
items.remediation: Define an EventType string enum or module-level constants in events.py. Replace string literals with constants. Low priority for Phase 1 but becomes important when external consumers subscribe to events.

items.title: executor.py re-exports arcllm.types.Message unnecessarily
items.description: executor.py line 15 has 'from arcllm.types import Message' with a comment '# Re-export for type reference'. This import is used only in the return type annotation of execute_tool_call() -> tuple[Message, bool]. The re-export creates a second import path for Message (arcrun.executor.Message vs arcrun._messages.Message). It also adds an unnecessary direct arcllm dependency to executor.py when _messages.py already serves as the arcllm adapter layer.
items.remediation: Import Message from arcrun._messages instead. This keeps _messages.py as the single arcllm adapter boundary. The type annotation is preserved; only the import path changes.

items.title: Default max_turns=25 hardcoded in two places
items.description: max_turns defaults to 25 in run() (line 22) and is extracted with options.get('max_turns', 25) in run_async() (line 83). The default is duplicated and not configurable via a module-level constant. If the default should change, both locations must be updated.
items.remediation: Define DEFAULT_MAX_TURNS = 25 constant. Both entry points reference it. Resolves naturally if TD-004 (extract _prepare_run) is addressed first.

items.title: No tool-level timeout in execute_tool_call
items.description: execute_tool_call() awaits tool_def.execute() with no timeout. A misbehaving tool can hang the entire run indefinitely. The SDD for spec 002 explicitly calls out tool-level timeout via asyncio.wait_for as a future enhancement point. Phase 3 (Recursive) will spawn child runs as tools -- a child run without timeout could deadlock the parent.
items.remediation: Add optional timeout_seconds to Tool dataclass (default None = no timeout). In execute_tool_call, wrap with asyncio.wait_for(tool_def.execute(...), timeout=tool_def.timeout_seconds) when set. Emit tool.timeout event on TimeoutError. Must be in place before Phase 3 Recursive strategy.

items.title: RunState.messages typed as list[Any] -- no message type safety
items.description: RunState.messages is list[Any] (state.py line 16). Messages flowing through the system are arcllm Message objects, but state has no type constraint. Any object can be appended to messages. This interacts with TD-001 (untyped model) -- without typed messages, the entire conversation flow is untyped.
items.remediation: Change to messages: list[Message] using the Message type from _messages.py. Requires TD-009 workaround (mypy ignore) since Message originates from untyped arcllm. Resolves naturally alongside TD-001.
